text:
The present article investigates the concept of transformation within the discipline of Biblical Spirituality. Biblical Spirituality bridges the terrain between the exegtical rigour of biblical studies on the one hand and the more descriptive, often historical, field of Christian Spirituality on the other. While transformation may evoke connotations related to political revolution and societal change or to more mundane self-help psychologies, the article will consider it primarily in terms of a spiritual shift— namely as a fundamental and existential change in the individual’s religious relation to God and thus in its ongoing fallout and impact upon that individual’s relationship to the world around him.

The discussion is carried out along two lines of thought: (a) the role of the Bible in the lives of both religious and non-religious individuals functioning as a change factor influencing beliefs and the basic ideas and values which underpin the socio-cultural structures even in societies which do not actively make use of the biblical text; and (b) the way the transformation processes described in the Bible are presented through the plot of narratives, through topos (recurring themes or subject matter in various narratives), and through historical or mystagogical frameworks. The common ground for both trends of thought underscores the fact that transformation is the living core of spirituality — the connecting element between ancient scriptural text and the ongoing experience of religious faith in the present. Viewed as an ongoing mutual process rather than an isolated event, the process of transformation guarantees that faith continues to be an effective living and life-affirming force in the personal and the social life of man.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
